section.title: Sennacherib and Hezekiah's Death
section.sectionRationale: Sennacherib invades Judah and mocks the LORD in letters and shouted taunts, but Hezekiah and Isaiah cry out in prayer and the LORD sends an angel to annihilate the Assyrian camp, delivering Jerusalem without a battle. Hezekiah's later pride during his illness and the visit of Babylon's ambassadors becomes a test, and though he humbles himself in time, the chapter closes with his death and a summary of his genuinely great, wholehearted reign.
section.thesis: The same king who saw God destroy an entire invading army in answer to prayer still had to learn, through the test of prosperity and a foreign embassy, that pride can creep in anywhere, showing that no past deliverance, however dramatic, exempts anyone from the ongoing need for humility.
section.frameNote: Four beats: Sennacherib's invasion and Hezekiah's preparations, verses 1 to 8; Sennacherib's blasphemous taunts, verses 9 to 19; the LORD's deliverance of Jerusalem, verses 20 to 23; and Hezekiah's illness, pride, and the summary of his reign and death, verses 24 to 33.

verses.text: After all these acts of faithfulness, Sennacherib king of Assyria came and invaded Judah. He laid siege to the fortified cities, intending to conquer them for himself.
When Hezekiah saw that Sennacherib had come to make war against Jerusalem,
he consulted with his leaders and mighty men about stopping up the waters of the springs outside the city, and they helped him carry it out.
Many people assembled and stopped up all the springs and the stream that flowed through the land. "Why should the kings of Assyria come and find plenty of water?" they said.

Then Hezekiah worked resolutely to rebuild all the broken sections of the wall and to raise up towers on it. He also built an outer wall and reinforced the supporting terraces of the City of David, and he produced an abundance of weapons and shields.
Hezekiah appointed military commanders over the people and gathered the people in the square of the city gate. Then he encouraged them, saying,
"Be strong and courageous! Do not be afraid or discouraged before the king of Assyria and the vast army with him, for there is a greater One with us than with him.
With him is only the arm of {{basar|flesh}}, but with us is the LORD our God to help us and to fight our battles." So the people were {{chazaq|strengthened}} by the words of Hezekiah king of Judah.

Later, as Sennacherib king of Assyria and all his forces besieged Lachish, he sent his servants to Jerusalem with a message for King Hezekiah of Judah and all the people of Judah who were in Jerusalem:
"This is what Sennacherib king of Assyria says: What is the basis of your confidence, that you remain in Jerusalem under siege?
Is not Hezekiah misleading you to give you over to death by famine and thirst when he says, 'The LORD our God will deliver us from the hand of the king of Assyria?'
Did not Hezekiah himself remove His high places and His altars and say to Judah and Jerusalem, 'You must worship before one altar, and on it you shall burn sacrifices'?
Do you not know what I and my fathers have done to all the peoples of the lands? Have the gods of these nations ever been able to deliver their land from my hand?
Who among all the gods of these nations that my fathers devoted to destruction has been able to deliver his people from my hand? How then can your God deliver you from my hand?

So now, do not let Hezekiah deceive you, and do not let him mislead you like this. Do not believe him, for no god of any nation or kingdom has been able to deliver his people from my hand or from the hand of my fathers. How much less will your God deliver you from my hand!"
And the servants of Sennacherib spoke further against the LORD God and against His servant Hezekiah.

He also wrote letters mocking the LORD, the God of Israel, and saying against Him: "Just as the gods of the nations did not deliver their people from my hand, so the God of Hezekiah will not deliver His people from my hand."
Then the Assyrians called out loudly in Hebrew to the people of Jerusalem who were on the wall, to frighten and terrify them in order to capture the city.
They spoke against the God of Jerusalem as they had spoken against the gods of the peoples of the earth, the work of human hands.
In response, King Hezekiah and the prophet Isaiah son of Amoz cried out to heaven in prayer,

and the LORD sent an angel who annihilated every mighty man of valor and every leader and commander in the camp of the king of Assyria. So he withdrew to his own land in disgrace. And when he entered the temple of his god, some of his own sons struck him down with the sword.
So the LORD saved Hezekiah and the people of Jerusalem from the hands of King Sennacherib of Assyria and all others, and He gave them rest on every side.
Many brought offerings to Jerusalem for the LORD and valuable gifts for Hezekiah king of Judah, and from then on he was exalted in the eyes of all nations.

units.label: Hezekiah's illness, pride, and death
units.note: Hezekiah humbled the pride of his heart

verses.text: In those days Hezekiah became mortally ill. So he prayed to the LORD, who spoke to him and gave him a sign.
But because his heart was proud, Hezekiah did not repay the favor shown to him. Therefore wrath came upon him and upon Judah and Jerusalem.
Then Hezekiah {{kana|humbled}} the pride of his heart, he and the people of Jerusalem, so that the wrath of the LORD did not come upon them during the days of Hezekiah.
Hezekiah had very great riches and honor, and he made treasuries for his silver, gold, precious stones, spices, shields, and all kinds of valuable articles.
He also made storehouses for the harvest of grain and new wine and oil, stalls for all kinds of livestock, and pens for the flocks.
He made cities for himself, and he acquired herds of sheep and cattle in abundance, for God gave him very great wealth.
It was Hezekiah who blocked the upper outlet of the Spring of Gihon and channeled it down to the west side of the City of David. And Hezekiah prospered in all that he did.

And so when ambassadors of the rulers of Babylon were sent to him to inquire about the wonder that had happened in the land, God left him alone to test him, that He might know all that was in Hezekiah's heart.
As for the rest of the acts of Hezekiah and his deeds of loving devotion, they are indeed written in the vision of the prophet Isaiah son of Amoz in the Book of the Kings of Judah and Israel.

And Hezekiah rested with his fathers and was buried in the upper tombs of David's descendants. All Judah and the people of Jerusalem paid him honor at his death. And his son Manasseh reigned in his place.

basar.gloss: Flesh. Hezekiah tells the people that Assyria has only an arm of basar, flesh, mere human strength, while Judah has the LORD Himself to fight their battles; the contrast between fallible flesh and the living God grounds the whole army's courage.

chazaq.gloss: To strengthen, be strong, or courageous. Hezekiah's exhortation, be strong and courageous, chazaq, echoes the LORD's own charge to Joshua, Joshua 1:9, tying this crisis to the same divine promise of presence that carried Israel into the promised land generations before.

kana.gloss: To humble, subdue, or bring low. When wrath comes because of his pride, Hezekiah kana, humbles, himself and the people, averting judgment during his own lifetime; the same root used of Judah's humbling under Ahaz here describes a self humbling that turns aside deserved wrath rather than provoking it.

basic.context: After Hezekiah's remarkable reforms, Assyria's powerful king Sennacherib invades Judah, and Hezekiah responds with both practical preparation, securing the water supply and strengthening the walls, and spiritual courage, reminding the people that Assyria has only human strength while Judah has the LORD Himself. Sennacherib's servants mock God directly in letters and shouted taunts, comparing the LORD to the powerless gods of conquered nations. Hezekiah and Isaiah cry out in prayer, and the LORD sends an angel who annihilates the Assyrian camp overnight, so that Sennacherib withdraws in disgrace and is later assassinated by his own sons. Yet Hezekiah's story does not end triumphantly without further testing: during a near fatal illness, pride creeps into his heart, and though he repents in time to avert immediate judgment, a subsequent visit from Babylonian ambassadors becomes a test God allows to reveal what is truly in his heart before the summary of his generally faithful reign and death.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative combining a dramatic account of miraculous deliverance with an honest, sobering account of the same king's subsequent pride. It is not suggesting that great faith in one crisis guarantees humility in every future test, but it does show that God both delivers dramatically in answer to prayer and continues to test even faithful hearts to expose what remains hidden within them.
basic.originalLanguages: Hezekiah contrasts Assyria's mere arm of basar, flesh, with the LORD who fights for Judah, urging the people to be chazaq, strong and courageous, language that deliberately echoes Joshua's own commissioning. Later, when pride rises in Hezekiah's heart during his illness, it is his willingness to kana, humble, himself that turns aside the wrath his pride had provoked, showing that humility remains necessary even after the greatest deliverances.
basic.typology: The angel who single handedly annihilates an entire invading army in response to prayer, without Judah needing to strike a single blow, previews the pattern of salvation accomplished entirely by God on behalf of a people who can only cry out and trust, fulfilled completely in the victory Christ wins over sin and death, a victory believers receive rather than achieve.
basic.christ: Hezekiah's confident declaration, with him is only the arm of flesh, but with us is the LORD our God to help us and to fight our battles, anticipates the gospel confidence that no merely human power, however vast, can prevail against the living God who fights for His people; this same confidence rests fully secure in Christ, who has already won the decisive victory over every enemy, visible and invisible, that could ever threaten His church. Hezekiah's later pride and needed humbling, even after such triumph, shows why every believer, however great their past experience of God's power, still needs the ongoing work of grace to keep pride from creeping back in.
basic.god: God is shown as the true and only reliable deliverer, personally destroying an entire army through a single angel while His people simply cry out in prayer, vindicating His own honor against Sennacherib's direct blasphemy. Yet God is equally shown testing even a faithful king's heart through prosperity and foreign attention, proving that His concern is not only for dramatic external deliverance but for the ongoing, hidden posture of the heart.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 36 to 37 gives the fuller prophetic account of this same siege and deliverance. Joshua 1:9 is the same charge to be strong and courageous that Hezekiah echoes to his people. 2 Kings 20:12-19 gives additional detail on the Babylonian embassy and Isaiah's resulting prophecy of exile. 2 Corinthians 12:7-9 shows Paul similarly needing continued humbling even after extraordinary spiritual experience, so that God's power would be perfected in weakness.
basic.oneStory: The LORD's total, effortless destruction of Sennacherib's blasphemous army in answer to simple prayer shows that no human power, however vast, can prevail against God on behalf of His people. Yet the same chapter's honest account of Hezekiah's subsequent pride and needed humbling reminds every reader that no past deliverance, however dramatic, exempts anyone from the ongoing, lifelong need for humility before the God who alone truly fights, saves, and searches every heart.

context.literary: The chapter is structured as two distinct but connected tests: a public, military crisis in which Hezekiah's faith and courage shine brightly, followed by a private, personal crisis of illness and pride in which his faith proves more fragile, giving a fuller, more honest picture of the same king than either episode alone would provide.
context.historical: Sennacherib's 701 BC invasion of Judah is well attested outside the Bible in Assyrian records, including his own Prism inscriptions boasting of shutting up Hezekiah like a caged bird in Jerusalem, though notably these Assyrian records never claim to have actually captured the city, consistent with the biblical account of a withdrawal rather than conquest.
context.canonical: The parallel accounts in 2 Kings 18 to 19 and Isaiah 36 to 39 give considerably more detail on both the military crisis and Hezekiah's illness, together forming one of the most extensively documented episodes in the entire history of the divided kingdom.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record both the LORD's dramatic, total deliverance of Jerusalem from Sennacherib's blasphemous invasion in response to prayer, and the honest, sobering account of Hezekiah's subsequent pride during his illness and the Babylonian embassy, showing that God tests even genuinely faithful hearts to reveal and correct what remains hidden within them.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter narrates a specific historical deliverance and a specific subsequent testing; it is not promising that every crisis will end in such dramatic rescue, nor that great faith in one moment guarantees humility in every future test. What endures is the pattern that God alone truly delivers His people, and that ongoing humility, not a single great act of trust, is what faithfulness over a lifetime requires.

debates.question: What was the purpose of God allowing the Babylonian ambassadors' visit and Hezekiah's proud response to it?

views.label: A genuine test to reveal Hezekiah's heart
views.case: Verse 31 explicitly states God left him alone to test him, that He might know all that was in Hezekiah's heart, suggesting God permitted this specific situation to expose, both to Hezekiah himself and to later readers, a pride that had not yet been fully addressed even after the earlier illness.

views.label: A consequence of pride already present from the earlier illness
views.case: The parallel account in 2 Kings 20:12-19 and Isaiah 39 shows Hezekiah proudly displaying all his treasures to the Babylonian envoys, suggesting this episode is less a fresh, isolated test and more the outworking of the same unaddressed pride noted already in verse 25, now manifesting concretely before a watching foreign power.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree the episode exposes real pride in Hezekiah's heart even after his earlier deliverance and repentance, and both recognize that this pride carried serious consequences, since Isaiah's resulting prophecy, 2 Kings 20:16-18, foretells the very Babylonian exile that would later befall Hezekiah's descendants.

study.originalLanguages: Hezekiah grounds his people's courage in the contrast between Assyria's mere arm of basar, flesh, and the LORD who fights for them, urging them to be chazaq, strong and courageous, the very words spoken over Joshua generations earlier. When pride later rises in his own heart, it is his choice to kana, humble himself, that turns aside the wrath his pride had provoked, a pattern of ongoing humility required even after the greatest of deliverances.

typology.type: An entire invading army destroyed by a single angel while God's people only pray and trust
typology.fulfillment: The victory over sin, death, and every hostile power accomplished entirely by Christ and received by faith rather than achieved by our own strength, Colossians 2:15.

typology.type: A faithful king still requiring humbling even after extraordinary deliverance
typology.fulfillment: The ongoing need for grace against pride even in mature believers, illustrated in Paul's own thorn in the flesh, 2 Corinthians 12:7-9.

study.christ: Hezekiah's declaration that Assyria has only an arm of flesh while Judah has the LORD Himself to fight their battles anticipates the gospel's own confidence that Christ has already triumphed over every hostile power through the cross, disarming the rulers and authorities and making a public spectacle of them, Colossians 2:15, so that believers face no threat, however vast it appears, that has not already been decisively defeated. Yet Hezekiah's subsequent pride, even after witnessing such total deliverance, shows the ongoing need every believer has for a Savior who not only rescues from external threat but continually humbles and refines the heart from within.
study.god: God proves Himself the only true and reliable deliverer, personally destroying an entire blasphemous army through a single angel while vindicating His own honor against Sennacherib's open mockery. Yet God is equally revealed as one who tests even faithful hearts through prosperity and attention, showing His concern reaches beneath external deliverance into the hidden, ongoing posture of the heart, and that He allows circumstances precisely to reveal, and then to correct, what pride would otherwise leave concealed.

crossReferences.note: The fuller prophetic account of Sennacherib's siege and the LORD's deliverance of Jerusalem, including Isaiah's own extended prophecy against Assyria.
Be strong and courageous, do not be afraid, the same charge Hezekiah echoes to encourage his people before Assyria's invasion.
Additional detail on the Babylonian embassy and Isaiah's resulting prophecy that Hezekiah's own descendants would be carried into Babylonian exile.
Christ's decisive triumph over every hostile power through the cross, the ultimate fulfillment of the deliverance Hezekiah witnessed at Jerusalem's walls.

study.oneStory: The LORD's total, effortless destruction of Sennacherib's blaspheming army, achieved through simple, believing prayer rather than Judah's own military might, shows that God alone truly saves His people from every threat, however overwhelming it appears. Yet the same chapter's honest record of Hezekiah's subsequent pride, and his necessary humbling, reminds every believer that no past victory, however dramatic, removes the lifelong need to walk humbly before the God who both delivers and searches the heart, a pattern completed in Christ, our final deliverer and the one who perfects His power in our ongoing weakness.

quiz.q: How did Hezekiah practically prepare Jerusalem for Sennacherib's invasion?

options.text: He stopped up the water springs outside the city, rebuilt and reinforced the walls, and produced weapons and shields.
options.note: Verses 3 to 5 describe these specific, practical preparations.

options.text: He relied entirely on prayer with no practical preparation whatsoever.
options.note: The text describes substantial practical preparations alongside, not instead of, spiritual trust and prayer.

options.text: He surrendered the city immediately without any defense.
options.note: The text describes extensive defensive preparations, the opposite of immediate surrender.

options.text: No preparations are described in the chapter.
options.note: Verses 3 to 8 give detailed, specific preparations.

quiz.q: What was the core message of Hezekiah's encouragement to the people before the siege?

options.text: That Assyria had only an arm of flesh, while Judah had the LORD their God to help them and fight their battles.
options.note: Verses 7 to 8 give this exact contrast that grounded the people's courage.

options.text: That Judah's army was simply larger and better equipped than Assyria's.
options.note: The stated basis for courage is trust in the LORD's help, not confidence in Judah's own military size or equipment.

options.text: That the people should attempt to negotiate a peace treaty with Sennacherib.
options.note: Hezekiah's message calls for courage grounded in trust in God, not a strategy of negotiation with the invading king.

options.text: No specific message of encouragement is recorded.
options.note: Verses 7 to 8 give an explicit, recorded message of encouragement.

quiz.q: How did the LORD ultimately deliver Jerusalem from Sennacherib's army?

options.text: In response to Hezekiah and Isaiah's prayer, the LORD sent an angel who annihilated every mighty man and leader in the Assyrian camp.
options.note: Verses 20 to 21 describe this direct, miraculous deliverance.

options.text: Through a long, hard fought battle in which Judah's army eventually prevailed.
options.note: The text describes a direct, miraculous act of the LORD through an angel, not a conventional, hard fought battle by Judah's own forces.

options.text: By Hezekiah paying a large ransom to Sennacherib.
options.note: The deliverance came through divine intervention in response to prayer, not through a ransom payment.

options.text: Jerusalem was not actually delivered from Sennacherib.
options.note: Verses 21 to 22 explicitly describe the LORD saving Hezekiah and the people of Jerusalem.

quiz.q: What happened when Hezekiah became mortally ill, and what was his response?

options.text: He prayed and received a sign from the LORD, but his heart became proud, so he humbled himself to avert wrath during his own lifetime.
options.note: Verses 24 to 26 record this full sequence of illness, pride, and subsequent humbling.

options.text: He remained humble and grateful throughout his entire illness with no pride at all.
options.note: The text explicitly states his heart became proud following the favor shown to him, requiring a subsequent act of humbling.

options.text: He refused to pray and rejected God's help entirely.
options.note: The text explicitly describes Hezekiah praying and receiving a sign from the LORD, not a rejection of God's help.

options.text: No illness of Hezekiah is described in this chapter.
options.note: Verses 24 to 26 give a detailed account of his illness and its aftermath.

quiz.q: Why did God allow the visit of the Babylonian ambassadors to Hezekiah?

options.text: To test him, that He might know all that was in Hezekiah's heart.
options.note: Verse 31 gives this exact, explicit purpose for the test.

options.text: To establish a permanent military alliance between Judah and Babylon.
options.note: The stated purpose is a test of Hezekiah's heart, not the formation of a political or military alliance.

options.text: To punish Hezekiah for his earlier faithfulness during Sennacherib's invasion.
options.note: The stated purpose is testing to reveal his heart, not punishment for prior faithfulness, which the text consistently commends.

options.text: No reason is given for the ambassadors' visit.
options.note: Verse 31 explicitly gives the reason for this test.

questions: Hezekiah told the people that Assyria had only an arm of flesh, while they had the LORD Himself. What overwhelming threat in your own life needs to be measured against the greater reality of who is truly with you?

Sennacherib's blasphemy was answered not with an argument but with prayer and God's direct action. When you face mockery or opposition to your faith, do you tend to respond with anxious arguing or with quiet, trusting prayer?

Even after witnessing God's dramatic deliverance, pride crept into Hezekiah's heart during a later season of blessing and illness. What season of blessing in your own life might be quietly growing pride you have not yet noticed?
Hezekiah humbled himself in time to avert further wrath. What would it look like for you to humble yourself now, proactively, rather than waiting until consequences force the issue?
God allowed a test specifically to reveal what was in Hezekiah's heart. Are you willing to ask God to search your own heart honestly, even if you fear what a genuine test might reveal?

translationNotes.note: Lachish was a heavily fortified city southwest of Jerusalem; Sennacherib's siege of Lachish is corroborated by the famous Lachish reliefs discovered in his palace at Nineveh, among the most detailed ancient depictions of a biblical era siege.

The parallel accounts in 2 Kings 19:35 and Isaiah 37:36 specify the number destroyed by the angel as 185,000, a detail not repeated in this Chronicles account but consistent with its record of the army's annihilation.

The fuller account of the Babylonian embassy appears in 2 Kings 20:12-19 and Isaiah 39, where Hezekiah's proud display of his treasuries to the envoys prompts Isaiah's prophecy that Judah's own wealth and descendants would one day be carried off to that very Babylon.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 2 Chronicles 32:1-33. No em dashes or en dashes appeared in the source text for this chapter.
